provenance.source_corpus: Riparian vegetation recovery in a 23 year chronosequence of clear-cuts along boreal headwater streams

source_spans.text: We investigated the impacts of clearcut harvesting on riparian plant communities and their recovery along headwater streams in boreal mixedwood forests of northwestern Ontario, Canada.
We found that clearcut harvesting had no immediate impact on overall species richness or diversity, but it was associated with changes in species composition.
Some late-seral species were locally eliminated and showed no sign of recovery 23 years after harvesting.
Richness of grasses was higher in clearcuts compared to downstream unharvested forests and reference forests.
Grass abundance was lowest at the upstream locations of reference forests and highest at early clearcuts.
Richness of mosses was significantly higher in age class 2 than in age class 1, which varied significantly between disturbance types, high in downstream unharvested forests and low in clearcuts
Compared to downstream unharvested forests, moss abundance was lower in the upstream clearcuts
We found that clearcut harvesting had no immediate impact on overall species richness or diversity
Rubus idaeus was the most dominant species in clearcuts of age 3– 7 years
The post-harvest habitats were often dominated by ruderal invasive species.
rapid reduction of light availability in post-clearcut successional communities results in their elimination
